Analysis: engagement trajectories and dropout risk

What chain-structure adds to a 3-state engagement model

The bundled trajectories dataset records the weekly engagement state of 138 students across 15 weeks of a course. Three states: Active, Average, Disengaged. Some students have a terminal-NA pattern in the matrix — once their data goes missing, it stays missing — which we treat as dropout. This is the cleanest dataset in the package for chain_structure() because it admits two complementary chain models:

  1. Engagement-only chain (3 states): how do students cycle between engagement levels? Regular, non-reversible — markov_stability()’s long-run distribution is interpretable.
  2. Augmented chain with Dropout as a fourth absorbing state: how long until a student drops out, conditional on current engagement? The three engagement states are now transient and markov_stability() is not the right summary — chain_structure() is.

Both models are needed for a complete reading. Two functions in the package handle the wide-data plumbing:

  • actor_endpoints(data) — tidy per-actor first/last state + dropout flag.
  • mark_terminal_state(data, state = "Dropout") — fill terminal NAs with an explicit label so the resulting chain has the label as a structurally absorbing state.

Setup

ep <- actor_endpoints(trajectories)
kt(data.frame(
  metric = c("students", "weeks observed", "dropouts (terminal NA)",
             "completers"),
  value  = c(nrow(ep), ncol(trajectories),
             sum(ep$dropped_out), sum(!ep$dropped_out)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
))
metric value
students 138
weeks observed 15
dropouts (terminal NA) 18
completers 120 
kt(head(ep, 6),
   caption = "actor_endpoints() — first six rows")
actor_endpoints() — first six rows
actor first_state last_state first_step last_step n_observed dropped_out
1 Active Active 1 12 12 TRUE
2 Average Average 1 15 15 FALSE
3 Average Active 1 15 15 FALSE
4 Active Average 1 15 15 FALSE
5 Active Average 1 15 15 FALSE
6 Average Average 1 15 15 FALSE

Each row records when the student entered the course (first_step, first_state), when they were last seen (last_step, last_state), and whether they dropped out (last_step < 15 with all subsequent weeks NA).

Part 1 — Engagement chain (no dropout)

build_network() on the raw matrix gives the engagement-only chain: the dynamics conditional on remaining in the course. NAs are skipped silently when transitions are counted.

net_eng <- build_network(as.data.frame(trajectories), method = "relative")
cs_eng  <- chain_structure(net_eng)
summary(cs_eng)
state classification period persistence return_probability sojourn_steps stationary_probability
Active recurrent 1 0.6976 1 3.31 0.3719
Average recurrent 1 0.6099 1 2.56 0.4431
Disengaged recurrent 1 0.4831 1 1.93 0.1850

Reading the summary: the chain is regular (irreducible plus aperiodic), so markov_stability()’s stationary distribution is interpretable. Disengaged is the least sticky state (persistence ≈ 0.48, sojourn ≈ 1.93 weeks); Active is the stickiest (persistence ≈ 0.70, sojourn ≈ 3.31). All three states return to themselves with probability 1 (return_probability = 1 for recurrent states by definition).

Long-run mass: ~37% Active, ~44% Average, ~19% Disengaged. Disengaged absorbs the smallest share of student-weeks.

kt(round(net_eng$weights, 3),
   caption = "Engagement transition matrix (rows = this week, cols = next)")
Engagement transition matrix (rows = this week, cols = next)
Active Average Disengaged
0.698 0.267 0.035
0.204 0.610 0.186
0.120 0.397 0.483

The off-diagonals show how each state is exited. From Disengaged, 40% recover to Average and 12% jump to Active. From Active, only 3.5% drop directly to Disengaged — the Active → Disengaged direct path is ten times less likely than Average → Disengaged, suggesting disengagement is reached gradually, not abruptly.

plot(cs_eng)

The heatmap is saturated dark across all 9 cells: every state is reachable from every other with probability 1. The diagonal pattern is the visual signature of non-reversibility — the asymmetric flow between Active, Average, and Disengaged.

Part 2 — Augmented chain with Dropout

mark_terminal_state(trajectories, state = "Dropout") fills every terminal NA with the label "Dropout". The resulting chain has Dropout as a structurally absorbing state.

M_aug   <- mark_terminal_state(trajectories, state = "Dropout")
net_aug <- build_network(M_aug, method = "relative")
cs_aug  <- chain_structure(net_aug)
summary(cs_aug)
state classification period persistence return_probability sojourn_steps absorption_probability mean_absorption_time
Active transient NA 0.6955 0.9745 3.28 1 101.01
Average transient NA 0.6050 0.9776 2.53 1 99.09
Disengaged transient NA 0.4687 0.9468 1.88 1 95.83
Dropout absorbing 1 1.0000 1.0000 Inf NA NA

The summary now tells a different story:

  • is_regular = FALSE — the chain is not regular because Dropout is absorbing. Running markov_stability() here would put all long-run mass on Dropout (correct as a limit, useless as a characterisation of student behaviour).
  • The three engagement states are classified transient, and Dropout is absorbing with period = 1 and infinite sojourn (once entered, never left).
  • absorption_probability is 1 for every transient state — every student eventually drops out under this model. That follows from Dropout being the only absorbing state and every transient state having a positive path to it.
  • mean_absorption_time is what differs by starting state.
kt(round(net_aug$weights, 3),
   caption = "Augmented transition matrix (Dropout column = per-week dropout probability)")
Augmented transition matrix (Dropout column = per-week dropout probability)
Active Average Disengaged Dropout
0.695 0.267 0.035 0.003
0.202 0.605 0.185 0.008
0.116 0.385 0.469 0.030
0.000 0.000 0.000 1.000

The Dropout column reads off the per-week dropout probability by current engagement state:

  • Active → Dropout: ~0.3% per week (~1 in 333 student-weeks)
  • Average → Dropout: ~0.8% per week
  • Disengaged → Dropout: ~3.0% per week (~1 in 33 student-weeks)

Disengaged students drop out at ~10× the rate of Active students. A single week of disengagement carries the immediate dropout risk of ten weeks of active engagement.

plot(cs_aug)

Visually, the Dropout column is solid dark (every state reaches Dropout with probability 1) and the Dropout row is white except in the Dropout column (once dropped, no return). The asymmetry of that row vs column is the signature of an absorbing state.

Why does starting state matter so little for absorption time?

The chain is forgiving. Even though Disengaged → Dropout per-week rate is 10× Active → Dropout, students mix between engagement states fast: from Disengaged, 52% are at Average or Active next week. The bad-week-now disadvantage is diluted within one or two steps.

Mathematically, mean_absorption_time integrates over future trajectories, weighted by transition probabilities. A Disengaged student’s expected dropout time is not the inverse of the immediate dropout rate (1/0.030 ≈ 33 weeks); it is the equilibrium-mixture-weighted rate (~1/0.0104 ≈ 96 weeks). The chain absorbs the short-run rate ratio into a long-run rate that is much closer across starting states.

This is a general property of regular transient cores embedded in absorbing chains — the long-run absorption rate is closer to the stationary mixture rate than to any individual state’s per-step rate. chain_structure() makes both the immediate rates (visible in the augmented transition matrix) and the absorbed-by rates (in mean_absorption_time) visible side by side.

Part 3 — Cross-tabulation: where were dropouts last seen?

The model-based view above has a model-free counterpart: simply cross-tabulate the per-actor last_state against the dropout flag.

tab <- with(ep[!is.na(ep$last_state), ],
            table(last_state, dropped_out, dnn = c("last_state", "dropped_out")))
kt(data.frame(
  last_state  = rownames(tab),
  dropouts    = as.integer(tab[, "TRUE"]),
  completers  = as.integer(tab[, "FALSE"]),
  pct_dropout = sprintf("%.1f%%",
                        100 * tab[, "TRUE"] /
                              (tab[, "TRUE"] + tab[, "FALSE"])),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
))
last_state dropouts completers pct_dropout
Active 2 43 4.4%
Average 6 56 9.7%
Disengaged 10 19 34.5%

Of students last seen Disengaged, ~35% dropped out. Of students last seen Average, ~10% dropped out. Of students last seen Active, ~5% dropped out. The ten-to-one ratio between Disengaged and Active matches the per-week transition-probability ratio computed via the chain. The two readings agree on the behavioural signal.

When to use which

Question Right summary
Long-run engagement balance, ignoring dropout markov_stability() on the engagement-only chain
Is the engagement chain regular? Does the stationary distribution exist? chain_structure()$is_regular on the engagement-only chain
Per-week dropout probability by current state P[i, "Dropout"] from build_network(mark_terminal_state(data))
Expected weeks to dropout from each state chain_structure()$mean_absorption_time on the augmented chain
Probability of eventual dropout chain_structure()$absorption_probabilities (= 1 here)
Per-actor first/last state and dropout flag actor_endpoints(data)
Backwards check: where were the dropouts last seen? Cross-tab last_state × dropped_out from actor_endpoints()

The two chain models — engagement-only and engagement-plus-Dropout — are complementary. The first describes the dynamics of the transient core; the second embeds those dynamics in a funnel toward absorption. chain_structure() is the diagnostic that chooses the right summary: when an absorbing state is present, markov_stability() is uninterpretable and the absorption analysis takes its place.

References

  • Kemeny, J. G. and Snell, J. L. (1976). Finite Markov Chains. Springer-Verlag.
  • Norris, J. R. (1997). Markov Chains. Cambridge University Press.
  • Spedicato, G. A. (2017). Discrete Time Markov Chains with R. R Journal 9(2), 84–104.
